label: ai
topic: Review of a horror film about sinister imaginary friends that lacks imagination
text:
There is a version of "The Friend" that works. You can see it in glimpses — in the unsettling first fifteen minutes, in the remarkable performance of its nine-year-old lead, in the premise itself, which takes the common childhood phenomenon of imaginary friends and asks: what if the friend is real, and what if it doesn't want to leave?

Unfortunately, that version exists mostly in potential. What we actually get is a frustratingly conventional horror exercise that mistakes exposition for atmosphere and jump scares for dread.

The setup is promising. Eight-year-old Lily, played with startling naturalism by newcomer Isla Reeves, moves with her recently widowed father to a house in rural Vermont. She begins talking to someone named Mr. Hollow — a tall, thin figure she describes in drawings that grow progressively more disturbing. Her father dismisses it as a grief response. The audience, having seen a horror film before, understands that Mr. Hollow is something worse than imaginary.

Reeves is genuinely extraordinary. She delivers lines to empty corners of rooms with a conviction that makes you involuntarily glance at the space beside her. When she tells her father that Mr. Hollow "doesn't like it when you talk about Mummy," the temperature drops. She is doing the work of a much better movie.

The problems begin in the second act, when director James Calloway loses faith in his premise and reaches for the toolkit of mainstream horror. We get the obligatory scene where a character researches the house's dark history on a laptop. We get the medium who provides convenient mythological context. We get a third-act revelation explained in a monologue so laden with backstory it could have been delivered via PowerPoint.

This is the fundamental error. The power of an imaginary friend story lies in ambiguity — in the space between what a child perceives and what is actually happening. "The Friend" collapses that space methodically, replacing uncertainty with lore, dread with plot mechanics. By the time Mr. Hollow is fully revealed in CGI that evokes nothing so much as a tall man in a good Halloween costume, the film has become indistinguishable from a dozen other supernatural horror entries.

The comparison that comes to mind, inevitably and unfavourably, is Jennifer Kent's "The Babadook," which explored similar territory with infinitely more restraint. Kent understood that the monster is most frightening when it is also a metaphor. Calloway understands monsters only as things that need to be shown, explained, and defeated in a climactic confrontation involving a lot of screaming and broken furniture.

David Oyelowo brings his usual gravity to the father, though the script gives him little beyond looking worried. Sarah Paulson appears as a therapist who exists primarily to articulate themes the film doesn't trust the audience to identify. "Children process grief in ways we don't always understand," she says, in case you somehow missed the previous forty minutes.

There are effective moments scattered throughout. A sequence involving Lily and a bathroom mirror demonstrates genuine command of spatial tension. A scene where she calmly sets a place at the dinner table for Mr. Hollow achieves the domestic uncanniness the rest of the film is chasing. These scenes succeed because they trust silence over explanation.

"The Friend" is not a bad film. It is a disappointing one, which is worse. Somewhere inside it is a quiet, devastating story about a lonely girl and the thing she invented to fill the space her mother left. That story deserved better.

Rating: 2 out of 5 stars.
